posts.name: Donald Pennet
posts.cooked:
Dauvissat Chablis1920×1440 197 KB

A few years ago, i would have been thrilled to have these wines and now it is just a nightmare. We could sell the Séchet and the La Forest for 70 € and the Chablis for 30 € but the prices listed on winesearcher for Germany are 165 € for the Sechet 173 € for La Forest (2020 vintage) and 100 € for the Chablis (2020 vintage). If I put them in the shop, one gets bombarded with emails from consumers who have never bought anything and if they were to buy these, would never buy anything again unless it is such trophies. The emails get more and more aggressive the more one turns down wannabee customers, Iget more and more frustrated and places like Winebeserkers become a great place to vent these frustrations,

I believe as the producer wants them to be sold, one must sell, it would be easy to horde these . But in my mind better not to have these bottles these days or sell to a champagne producer at the correct prices and in turn have them bump up the allocation.

posts.name: Russell Faulkner
posts.cooked:
Last time you bought this up I mentioned my own approach which is to package them up with other high quality wines in at normal margins such that the total package price is at least the market value of the rare wines. (Offering to good customers first).

I’d add that it’s amazing how rude and entitled some of the message one gets is. Good use of the block functions.

posts.name: Donald Pennet
posts.cooked:
Bundling has advantages and disadvantages. A customer made me aware of this. Customers sometimes buy bundles and then keep the bottle they want and sell the rest. They make a loss but probbaly pay less than they would, if they had bought at extortionate prices. One just has to look at Idealwine to see how many bottles are listed from current releases.

It is the same with setting a minimum turnover per customer. There is one dealer in Germany, who sets the limit at 15000 €. Customers have told me they buy and sell to get above the limit.

posts.name: Mikael B E K E L E
posts.cooked:
I think Russel’s approach works for special wines like this. The value approach to get access is in a way transparent and logical but perhaps with less heart (and it’s a business).

Another very fair way of doing it is to offer the wines to the same customers who kept buying the wines over the years (and if someone drops off offer the wines to the next in line good customers who you feel enjoys and like the wines).

I benefit from the latter (a bottle or two of each bottling) the retail could if he wished, sell the wines for 7-10 times the prices he sells it me. I always buy some of his other wines, and I hope that shows appreciation to him.

If you work with restaurants that are good customers and offer the wines at fair prices then that another route I would consider if I were in your shoes.

Those bottles can be a blessing, at least as long as you don’t dangle them in-front of a bunch of wine folks (without offering a bottle or two).
posts.display_username: Mikael B E K E L E

posts.name: S_Yæt€s
posts.cooked: You should keep a separate mailing list of your regular customers, maybe separate lists from that of folks who like specific categories. Could even cross-reference these lists with information from your point of sale software about who has purchased Dauvissat in the past.

posts.name: Mikko Tuomi
posts.cooked: I’ll take them all and video myself drinking them, hows that? I’ll leave a $1000 bond (to show Im not a flipper) that you return after I post the video.

posts.name: Fred Bower
posts.cooked:
Mikael_OB:

Another very fair way of doing it is to offer the wines to the same customers who kept buying the wines over the years (and if someone drops off offer the wines to the next in line good customers who you feel enjoys and like the wines).

This is how I have observed most trophies/unicorns get handled in the retail channels I’m familiar with. This loyal customer appreciates the opportunity, even if it is just a bottle or two.

Cheers,
fred

reply_to_user.name: Mikael B E K E L E

posts.name: Ian Sutton
posts.cooked:
Most egalitarian way to sell them?

Put them quietly up on the shelves one day, not on the same day as each other (to avoid someone cleaning the shelves out in one go). No fanfare, and definitely not online.

If someone is browsing and finds them, it is because they are specifically looking for Chablis / white burgundy, hence more likely to be bought by someone genuinely interested in the wines, than the prestige, or making a quick buck or 50 by flipping.

posts.name: Russell Faulkner
posts.cooked:
I always allocate a few bottles of rare stuff for people who want to come to the shop and buy them rather than online.

I’d happily not have a website but we wouldn’t be viable.

posts.name: Tom Blach
posts.cooked: It’s all a bit tiresome. The Dauvissat wines are good but not worth making a huge fuss about, there is plenty of Chablis that is just as good; though not much that is to my taste these days, nor that will last in the way that was once its special signature.

posts.name: Ian Sutton
posts.cooked:
Tom_Blach:

It’s all a bit tiresome. The Dauvissat wines are good but not worth making a huge fuss about, there is plenty of Chablis that is just as good; though not much that is to my taste these days, nor that will last in the way that was once its special signature.

I’ve seen it with a number of producers, across different regions, where a perfectly good, perhaps slightly under-appreciated producer, becomes the latest darling of a leading critic, and/or indeed gets talked up on wine forums. The prices escalate rapidly and what might have been decent value (and that value very much a part of the appeal), becomes nonsensically awful value, driven by groupthink / hype train, though presumably the buyers are happy they’ve got some wine from the ‘hot new star’. I found this especially daft with La Ca’Nova in Barbaresco, a solid (if a little foursquare / tannic) producer whose wines went unnoticed for many years. Galloni then talked them up, and all of a sudden they were hipster wines

posts.name: Rodrigo Br@g@
posts.cooked:
I feel like this topic has been discussed at nauseam here on Berserkers in some form or another.

There’s no perfect way to sell and allocate rare wines that one gets in limited quantities. No matter which approach one takes, they are bound to get complaints from one customer or another. No store gets enough of these wines to make all of their customers happy.

Do what makes sense for your store and lets you soundly sleep at night and let the chips fall where they may.

As for the issue of reselling wine, there’s no real way to get around that, for better or worse, the secondary market is the sole form of access to many for some of these rare wines, so it’s something that’s here to stay

posts.name: Donald Pennet
posts.cooked:
I agree that this topic has been discussed ad nauseam but there is to my mind no satsifactory solution.

In my mind, it is a bit like what I would call the Keller syndrome. If I were to sell cheap and know the customer would drink the bottles and they offered continual business no problem. But if I sell cheap and they are flipped, why should the second party make the big money. I totally understand a producer wants his part of profits which in effect leads to Teufelskreis or a vicious circle. I totally understand say the likes of Egly or Keller wanting a bigger share of the profits but in turn with such horrendous prices the wines or in Egly’s case champagne becomes obsolete or only accessible to a small minority as Galloni has said in a Liv-Ex interveiw, they have the necessary cash to buy the wines but not necessarily the taste to truly appreciate them.

The so called fine wine drinker (trophy hunters) faction hates bundling but it seems the lesser evil.

posts.name: Rodrigo Br@g@
posts.cooked:
Keller and Egly pricing are a function two separate causes. In Keller’s case, the price increases have mostly been a function of very large demand for the wines. The price of the wines ex-cellar continue to be modest. And as much as prices for Keller GGs have climbed in the secondary market, they have seen on average a decline from their high a few years ago and seem to have settled at a steady price. In Egly’s case the price increases seem to be for the most part due to price increases at the domaine itself. Whether you find the wines worth the ex-cellar or subsequent retail prices or not is a matter personal preference.

As far as reselling goes, as I said, it’s a function of the realities of life. The secondary market exists because some don’t have access to the wines via a primary source. Even the likes DRC, who probably takes some of the strictest measures to prevent resellers still can’t avoid it. One can’t really have their cake and eat it too. If a store sells an allocated wine for less than the market/wine-searcher price, it’s acknowledging the risk of there being some flippers. The minute they raise the price to the ‘market price’, they reduce the chances of flippers, but risk frustrating some customers. Each comes with benefits and downsides. One can’t just take the benefits of both approaches with the downside of neither.

A note on the Galloni comment, one start leaning into Aesop’s fox territory when they begin to cast judgement on who is and isn’t fit to enjoy a wine. I’ve had some great, rare and expensive wines shared with me when I was just starting out my journey. Could I necessarily appreciate them fully at the time? If I’m being honest, probably not. Nonetheless, they were memorable and formative part of my wine education. Conversations over who should and shouldn’t drink a wine based on a perceived notion of their wine experiences and potential appreciated of said wine just reeks of snobbery to me.

posts.name: yaacovbarselah
posts.cooked:
D_Pennet:

I agree that this topic has been discussed ad nauseam but there is to my mind no satsifactory solution.

perhaps take this observed trend as a directional signal?

posts.name: Andreas Scheppler
posts.cooked:
Rodrigo_B:

If a store sells an allocated wine for less than the market/wine-searcher price, it’s acknowledging the risk of there being some flippers. The minute they raise the price to the ‘market price’, they reduce the chances of flippers, but risk frustrating some customers. Each comes with benefits and downsides. One can’t just take the benefits of both approaches with the downside of neither.

Many wineries do not allow retailers to sell at market prices. If a DRC retailer were to sell at market prices, they would lose their allocation. So in reality, it’s either sell at allocation prices and live with some flippers, or sell at market prices and lose the allocation.

posts.name: larry schaffer
posts.cooked: The Bourbon sellers in the US are quite amused with this thread

posts.name: Tom Blach
posts.cooked: Raveneau’s wines are better, but pretty uneven. Louis Michel and Duplessis make wines much more to my taste and there are sometimes really great bottles from others which unfortunately rarely stay the course. There’s nothing clever about age for its own sake, of course, but modern styles tend to the fruity and boring when released even from top producers. If we wish to enjoy Chablis nowadays we have to accept that it is an entirely different wine to what it was in the twentieth century. I tend not to be terribly impressed, which means that I drink it much less than previously- which obviously means that my knowledge is now pretty patchy.

posts.name: Gavin.Curd
posts.cooked: I agree with what a lot of others have said. In the whiskey/bourbon business some stores will have a “back room” with their allocated bottles. They offer them to regulars who purchase often. Some do raffles, but I rather like the “back room” as a sort of benefit for supporting the local shop. Offering them to buyers you trust and for people who support the business is a great way to keep people coming back and saves a lot of headaches. In the end, the secondary market is what it is. Anything that is desired and rare can cause issues.

posts.name: Brian Befano
posts.cooked:
I agree holding for valued customers is a reasonable solution. The other reasonable solution is for stores to sell at full market price. While I rarely pay full market price for bourbon, I do appreciate that there is a store nearby that always has everything in stock, and why should they give a flipper the profit margin instead of taking it themselves.

The worst option appears to be what the OP is doing, listing publicly way below market and then complaining when people aggressively come out of the woodwork. Economics principles are no less real than Newton’s laws of motion.

posts.name: Benjamin Leth Andersen
posts.cooked:
I would say it depends on what your objective is: Are you looking to maximize profits, or are you on the side of the producers who wants their wines to be enjoyed and shared rather than used as investment vehicles.

If it is the former, the solution is easy; you either jack up the prices to WS level, or you bundle it with a lot of the slow-moving high margin stuff.

For the latter, the solution is in my mind equally ease, albeit takes more work; you allocate based on relationships (with individuals and restaurants) to maximize the likelihood that the wines gets to be enjoyed rather than flipped. Can you completely eliminate any financial speculation with this approach? Obviously not, but I believe that you can eliminate a large portion of it.

To me the latter is the most ‘pure’ and ‘romantic’ way to do things, honoring the wish of the producers that there wines gets to be enjoyed. However, I also fully acknowledge the need to drive a profitable business, so depending on the overall health of the operation it might not be feasible.

The latter is how the Danish importer of a bunch of the best Burgundy producers (A. and B. Ente, Coche-Dury, Mugneret-Gibourg, E. Rouget, Prieure Roch + non-burgundy Rayas) works - and I have quite a profound respect for that.

posts.name: Larry Link
posts.cooked:
Tom_Blach:

Raveneau’s wines are better, but pretty uneven.

Tom,

What do you mean by uneven? I have opened 15-20 bottles of Raveneau per year for the last 5 years, and in my experience they are consistently excellent. Mostly 04 to 17 vintage, but some from the 90s and all the way back to 88. It’s my most reliable white.

posts.name: Tom Blach
posts.cooked: Simply that some have been luckier than others and that 15-20 bottles is statistically a possible number for that luck to have held; but apart from that that many bottles when in perfect condition do not seem as exciting as others do.

posts.name: Larry Link
posts.cooked:

Larry, do you like the “botrytis” vintages of Raveneau?

Greg,

I assume you’re referring to vintages like 01, 13 and 16 where there was some rain around harvest in Chablis? While they are not my favorite vintages I appreciate that some botrytis can add aromatic complexity and an interesting mouth feel to the wines.

I do value the nuances between different vintages as it adds to the fun of this hobby. Raveneau in ripe years like 05, 09 and 10(which can show up to 2 grams/liter of residual sugar) are a great foil for cracked crab or lobster, whereas classic years like 04, 07, 08 and 14 scream for oysters or scallops.

When Tom mentioned that Raveneau for him was “uneven”, I (incorrectly) read that he had flawed wines from Raveneau. He clarified that he meant some bottles were more exciting than others, which I understand.

Given the uneven results opening most White Burgundy due to premox, for me Raveneau is the surest bet in my cellar. I’ve opened 5 Raveneaus that I classify as flawed (4 corked, 1 premoxed), out of some 200 bottles.

Raveneau in ripe years like 05, 09 and 10(which can show up to 2 grams/liter of residual sugar) are a great foil for cracked crab or lobster, whereas classic years like 04, 07, 08 and 14 scream for oysters or scallops.

I guess what I call “nuances between different vintages” you would call inconsistencies?

When Tom mentioned that Raveneau for him was “uneven”, I (incorrectly) read that he had flawed wines from Raveneau. He clarified that he meant some bottles were more exciting than others, which I understand.

Given the uneven results opening most White Burgundy due to premox, for me Raveneau is the surest bet in my cellar. I’ve opened 5 Raveneaus that I classify as flawed (4 corked, 1 premoxed), out of some 200 bottles.

Larry, I think it depends on what one means by consistency. If you mean “not flawed” from the perspective of premox, then I certainly agree - Raveneau, as well as Coche, are standouts in that department. But while I find Coche to be marvelously consistent, I do not find that with Raveneau at all from a winemaking perspective because it isn’t really made to be; I find aged botrytis vintages in particular undrinkable. And I do not think this is a “take what nature gives you” approach to winemaking - I’ve never had a bottle of Dauvissat taste like that. To me it reflects a winemaking philosophy at Raveneau that can, in the right vintages produce absolutely spectacular wines, but, in the wrong vintages, produce wines I don’t want to drink.
To be clear, I am not suggesting this makes either better than the other, but I definitely find Dauvissat more consistent. That said, I personally don’t find repurposing botrytis vintages for completely different pairings a persuasive argument for the viability of those wines anymore than the usefulness of premoxed wine as cooking sherry. That is, of course, just my opinion

Raveneau, as well as Coche, are standouts in that department

We are veering way off topic in true Berserkers fashion. I’m not bothered by the botrytis in the few vintages (01, 13 and 16 by my recollection) where I have tasted it. If I was, it would be easy enough to avoid those wines.

Aren’t you bothered by the premox rate that you experience with Dauvissat? To me, that “inconsistency” is much more prevalent, much more random and the wines are undrinkable and as you suggest unusable for even cooking.

I experienced an alarming rate of premox with Dauvissat, if I remember correctly it was in the neighborhood of 15%, out of 107 bottles opened. For comparison, since 2009 I’ve opened 211 bottles of Raveneau and have had 1 that was premoxed (1999 MdT).

posts.name: Robert M yers
posts.cooked:
I think you might be overthinking this. If you can’t think of 3 good customers to just offer a bottle to then maybe hide in plain sight one at a time on the shelf. Maybe put a note on the back, “sold as a pair with a similarly priced bottle”
I do think it would be rude to walk out with just that single.

I once had a retailer offer me a rare wine, I then said I’d like to buy a couple of other bottles in the same price range. After I looked around I asked if they had another bottle I would fill a case. We were both very happy.

posts.name: D@vid K@ntr0witz
posts.cooked:
Robert_M_yers:

I think you might be overthinking this. If you can’t think of 3 good customers to just offer a bottle to then maybe hide in plain sight one at a time on the shelf. Maybe put a note on the back, “sold as a pair with a similarly priced bottle”
I do think it would be rude to walk out with just that single.

If you don’t want people buying it, don’t put it on the shelf! I don’t care how retailers choose to allocate rare bottles, but if it’s on the shelf (or listed online for all-comers), it’s fair game.

posts.name: Robert M yers
posts.cooked:
Of course it is, that’s why you might put one out at a time. I guess you could put it online with a one bottle limit and then charge the same shipping for one bottle as a case

You want to drive foot traffic then you should have interesting things on the shelf.

There is No BeerSearcher.com . Same situation used to happen here with beer. But if you hide EVERY allocated beer and only sell to your favorites, people will stop coming in just to see what’s new. The hunt is part of the fun and if you eliminate any possibility you will lose traffic.

posts.name: Donald Pennet
posts.cooked:
Gold? Yellow, glittering, precious gold? No, gods, I am no idle votarist. Roots, you clear heavens! Thus much of this will make black white, foul fair, wrong right, base noble, old young, coward valiant. Act IV, Scene III Timon of Athens.

In consideration of all the posts here, I think the best solution is to sell to porducers with the result of bumping up allocations. We do the same with Coche and Rayas and in the end consumers profit with better prices and better allocations of champagnes. I would rather have one of the Brochets or Savart than the chablis.

I think the dilemna is who to sell it to, with these small allocations the only real way to sell is through lottery and even that has its problems.

with these small allocations the only real way to sell is through lottery

that’s what I suggested the last time you posed this question. Thought we had to come up with something new.
I didn’t even know selling to other producers would possibly be a thing.
I am glad I’m done chasing labels!

posts.name: Robert Alfert
posts.cooked:
Robert_M_yers:

I think you might be overthinking this. If you can’t think of 3 good customers to just offer a bottle to then maybe hide in plain sight one at a time on the shelf. Maybe put a note on the back, “sold as a pair with a similarly priced bottle”
I do think it would be rude to walk out with just that single.

I once had a retailer offer me a rare wine, I then said I’d like to buy a couple of other bottles in the same price range. After I looked around I asked if they had another bottle I would fill a case. We were both very happy.

My thoughts as well. Chambers essentially does this, offers things like Gonon to its long-standing customers that love and buy Northern Rhônes of all types, year after year. Surprisingly, a couple years ago I was dropped from their Rougeard list, surprising considering how much Loire I bought from them. But when I stepped back and thought about it, I’m sure I am a small fish compared to their more regular NYC customers. I probably buy more from Chambers than most other retailers, but there are obvious big guns in that city.

Back to the shelf comment, I walked into a very small wine store in Rosemary Beach, Florida, in March of 2020 (right when Covid hit) and immediately saw a single bottle of Rayas on a top shelf, in a row of other nice Rhônes, including Clape. I did not grab it initially, started talking with the owner, and eventually mentioned that I was surprised he had a Rayas on the shelf. His response was pretty cool, he gets a small allocation, feeds most to his best customers, and puts a few from the range on the shelf. Then he asked me to guess the price, I said $850. He said $375. I said “I’ll take it!” It was the glorious 2008. Best purchase I made in years, and I also bought a handful of other things, mostly as a gesture. And then came back the next day, gifted him a nice Rhone and bought more.

posts.name: Lasse Knudsen

One of the sites I buy from started giving out “allocation” points per X spend €. The more rare the bottle is, the more points are needed. You need to buy a decent amount of wine to get one allocated bottle, so buying to flip so you can get enough points would be a real hassle.

For me this works great. I use the site for some of my early purchases like Baudry and Marnes Blanches. And that will give me enough points to get a fun bottle or two, every year, at a decent price.

posts.name: Greg Kahn
posts.cooked:
Larry_Link:
Greg_K:

Larry, I think it depends on what one means by consistency. If you mean “not flawed” from the perspective of premox, then I certainly agree - Raveneau, as well as Coche, are standouts in that department. But while I find Coche to be marvelously consistent, I do not find that with Raveneau at all from a winemaking perspective because it isn’t really made to be; I find aged botrytis vintages in particular undrinkable. And I do not think this is a “take what nature gives you” approach to winemaking - I’ve never had a bottle of Dauvissat taste like that. To me it reflects a winemaking philosophy at Raveneau that can, in the right vintages produce absolutely spectacular wines, but, in the wrong vintages, produce wines I don’t want to drink.
To be clear, I am not suggesting this makes either better than the other, but I definitely find Dauvissat more consistent. That said, I personally don’t find repurposing botrytis vintages for completely different pairings a persuasive argument for the viability of those wines anymore than the usefulness of premoxed wine as cooking sherry. That is, of course, just my opinion

We are veering way off topic in true Berserkers fashion. I’m not bothered by the botrytis in the few vintages (01, 13 and 16 by my recollection) where I have tasted it. If I was, it would be easy enough to avoid those wines.

Aren’t you bothered by the premox rate that you experience with Dauvissat? To me, that “inconsistency” is much more prevalent, much more random and the wines are undrinkable and as you suggest unusable for even cooking.

I experienced an alarming rate of premox with Dauvissat, if I remember correctly it was in the neighborhood of 15%, out of 107 bottles opened. For comparison, since 2009 I’ve opened 211 bottles of Raveneau and have had 1 that was premoxed (1999 MdT).

Larry, as I said in my post, it depends on what you mean by consistency. I would not buy older Dauvissat without a photo of the bottle in front of some light because of premox issues. But if you view consistency in wine purely from the perspective of flaws, why aren’t you just buying post-DIAM Fevre which is subject neither to cork taint nor premox?

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
Very amusing, Donald, but the fact remains that this thread began with you deliberating whether to charge a 3x mark up or a 7x mark up on the ex-domaine price (assuming you have a direct allocation and aren’t a grey market flipper, which wouldn’t surprise me), and then lamenting the price of Burgundy.

You can criticize me as much as you like, and that may even be salutary; but I confess it does genuinely irritate me when you attack producers like Dauvissat who continue to honor their covenant with their historic clientele, in the face of rampant flipping and speculation, and frankly at the expense of their own best interests.

Small artisanal producers are the thin red line between us and a world of homogenized, soulless wine, where a handful of conglomerates produce a huge but illusory diversity of labels, much as you can see in the case of contemporary Scotch whisky. You are going after the wrong people here. However, perhaps given the economic situation in Germany we can forgive you.

Sure, I hosted a tasting for a dozen people last year with some bottles of Dom Pérignon—that we bough—back to the 1960s. If you had been there you would have heard me be very candid about what Dom Pérignon is and was, in terms of vineyard sourcing, production volume, positioning and so on. Some of the wines were excellent. Beyond that, well, I don’t always write my own captions/blurbs on our website.

And yes, if I get up in the morning with the desire to write about wine, it’s because I think there are values worth fighting for in the world of wine. That these values don’t happen to include thinking that the Western European middle classes should be able to roll up in their VWs and fill up the trunk with wines that cost no more than they did 20 years ago is of course where we differ.

posts.name: Donald Pennet
posts.cooked:
Small artisanal producers are the thin red line between us and a world of homogenized, soulless wine, where a handful of conglomerates produce a huge but illusory diversity of labels, much as you can see in the case of contemporary Scotch whisky. You are going after the wrong people here. However, perhaps given the economic situation in Germany we can forgive you.

I would draw on what Peter Leim said, scale of production does not inherently define its quality or approach. Small-scale producers may not always adhere to artisanal methods, and conversely, larger producers can maintain high-quality, artisanal standards. I think this is fundamental when understanding the wine industry.

There is no question, critics like yourself play a significant role in defining market perception and pricing. I would argue, it is essential to keep fine wine available for all segments of society rather than something eclusive for the few. My feeling is, you are basically making fine wine unattainable for the middle class.

This then brings the question of sustainability of market trends, who will support the wine industry if there is no middle class consumer base.

That these values don’t happen to include thinking that the Western European middle classes should be able to roll up in their VWs and fill up the trunk with wines that cost no more than they did 20 years ago is of course where we differ.

Again a statement where one could hold a discussion. Who supported your beloved Ulysse Collin when no critic offered patronage, the middle class, the same can be said of so many producers. As a producer I would be very careful about alienating the middle class and jumping on the William Kelley bandwagon.

PS I take it, you do not like germans.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
Did I make any of these wines unaffordable, Donald? Robert Parker Wine Advocate - Unbiased Wine Reviews

Sometimes the market follows, sometimes it doesn’t. I have some very unfashionable and inexpensive wines in my cellar that many snobs would disdain to drink, and I write about such wines, too. Meanwhile, you simultaneously claim to disdain the great wines you disparage as existing in the sphere of “luxury”, while also railing at the fact that you can’t afford them.

Economically wine today is very complicated. People are drinking less alcohol, and my generation and younger consumers still are not automatically gravitating towards wine. Producers are being paid to rip up their vineyards in Bordeaux. Is the future really mass market “beverage wines”? Does it even make sense ecologically to transport such products far from their region of production? All these and other questions seem pertinent. What is clear is that the market for wine with, let’s say, aspirations to greatness is likely to be more robust, durable, and interesting for producers.

link_counts.title: Robert Parker Wine Advocate - Unbiased Wine Reviews

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
Not only do I drink Bourgogne Rouge, I actually produce some

If you look at my instagram, you will see a lot of things, from old Auxey-Duresses (not from d’Auvenay, no) and Saint-Romain, to Bandol, Chinon, village wines from unheard of producers… Overall I try to focus on older wines as I think that’s an important part of our wine culture that it would be a shame to loose. And yes, there is plenty of sought after, expensive wine, too… Coche, Roumier, and so on. If people have listened when I have talked about wines from less prestigious appellations such as Guffens or Dureuil-Janthial, I suspect it is partly because I am credible when it comes to the historic benchmarks, too.

And along with the bottles, I venture to add, you will find an awful lot of entirely free content, if you want to learn about mildew, or machine harvesting, or this history of appellations and the construction of terroirs, or how wine presses work, and so on.

Anyway, further exchanges will have to wait for another time, I have some tastings to do this morning!

posts.name: Ian Sutton
posts.cooked:
D_Pennet:

I think this lack of balance says a lot. You are not a man of the people.

I don’t imagine any wine critic to be a man or woman of the people. They aren’t running their business to be generous in advice. They are running it to monetise their opinions. So picking one critic out for criticism feels churlish, and your accusation that he dislikes Germans is something that I’d strongly urge you to withdraw. You attacked him, he’s defending himself. Nothing more.

There are valid criticisms to be made of the effect critics have on the wine industry, driven by the sheep-like following of their subscribers. Their pronouncements can lead to utterly ridiculous price rises, through people **blindly following points scores.

** ironically I find that whilst the worst excesses of this may take some wines out of the reach of ordinary people, conversely it will leave some great value to be had for those prepared to trust their own palates, and explore wines not on the critics’ radar.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked: A lot of costs went up lately… but you can see today’s costs for the Côte d’Or in my post. If you want to do fewer hours per hectare of manual work, or do synthetic systemic treatments instead of organic, or do élevage in tank, or use cheaper corks, you can bring down the number. You can also bring it down by doing lots of it yourself and not taking much of a paycheck. Vincent has been investing for two decades and now the wines are valorized at their proper level. Before, relative to the quality and the work, they were underpriced. That is not sustainable for producers (would you like to work without a raise for a decade?) and so consumers who appreciate wines of that quality need to be prepared to pay for it—and these remain very reasonable. If you have been following them for a long time, no doubt you have large stocks acquired for very modest prices to console you for the fact that they are now a little more expensive.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked: There is a difference between being a consumer advocate and “a man of the people”, whatever that means in this context. I derive immense joy from wine, but I am not really sure how anyone can represent pushing an expensive consumer good with proven negative health implications as an unmitigated social good. No one should be excluded from drinking wine by constructing artificial barriers of snobbery, language etc, but I am also not trying to convince people to drink alcohol. Moreover, the “people” to which Donald refers is in fact just a tiny subset of people like him.

posts.name: Jürgen Steinke
posts.cooked: And your praise does not play a role?

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked: My praise has perhaps contributed to consumers discovering how good the wines are and being prepared to pay for them. Something Vincent deserved. We are talking about very minor price increases ex-domaine for wines that started out very inexpensive. Do you really begrudge someone working 20 years and finally being able to sell an exceptional product for 22 EUR?

Yes, a nice marketing pitch by him (and indeed other wine critics before and after). Meanwhile those that blindly followed his points ended up paying more for the wine than if he’d never anointed it with a high 90s score… and they even paid him money to guide them to wines that would end up overpriced through the sheep-like following. Marketing / business model genius.

posts.name: Howard Cooper

If you have wines that you want your best customers to get, you need to take steps to offer them to your best customers.

If you put them on the internet, who would you think would end up buying them - your best customers (who probably actually have lives) or people who spend a decent part of their days looking up prices for favored wines on Wine-Searcher?

If you want things to work out the way you want them to, you need to be less passive and more active.

The use of scores out of 100 and the recognition that higher scores got him greater brand promotion. Yes both were very effective and profitable marketing tools (I don’t like the word scam).
